meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) circuits + magnetism items. Stems and four options scraped verbatim from OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e Ch. 20-22 'Test Prep for AP Courses' single-best-answer multiple-choice items. OpenStax publishes the answer key to instructors only, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text or by direct calculation (answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text). 'Select two answers', five-option, figure-dependent, and free-response items were skipped. cpbs-xp132 states the resistor configuration (parallel) explicitly, consistent with the source figure and the only physically consistent answer choice.

questions.stem: Which of the following can be explained on the basis of conservation of charge in a closed circuit consisting of a battery, resistor, and metal wires?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of electrons leaving the battery will be equal to the number of electrons entering the battery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of electrons leaving the battery will be less than the number of electrons entering the battery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of protons leaving the battery will be equal to the number of protons entering the battery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of protons leaving the battery will be less than the number of protons entering the battery.

questions.explanation: Charge is conserved and the conduction charges are electrons (protons don't move through wires). In a closed loop, electrons are not created or consumed, so the number leaving the battery equals the number returning to it.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 20 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (charge conservation; electrons carry current).

hints.prompt: What does conservation of charge imply about the flow of charge around a closed circuit loop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Charge is neither created nor destroyed, so as much charge leaves a point as returns to it.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Charge steadily accumulates inside the battery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Charge is created by the resistor as current flows.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charge disappears whenever it does work.

hints.rationale: Conservation of charge means the charge entering the circuit equals the charge leaving it.

hints.prompt: In a metal wire, which particles actually move to carry the current, and are they created or destroyed in the loop?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electrons move, and they are conserved — not created or destroyed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Protons move through the wire and are conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Electrons are consumed by the resistor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Protons are created at the battery.

hints.rationale: Conduction current in metals is carried by electrons, which are conserved.

hints.prompt: Because protons are fixed in the metal lattice and do not flow, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The two choices that describe protons leaving and entering the battery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two choices that describe electrons leaving and entering the battery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only the choice about equal electron counts.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: Protons do not travel through wires, so both proton-based statements are out.

questions.stem: When a battery is connected to a bulb, there is 2.5 A of current in the circuit. What amount of charge will flow through the circuit in a time of 0.5 s?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 1 C

questions.explanation: Current is charge per unit time, I = Q/t, so Q = It = (2.5 A)(0.5 s) = 1.25 C.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 20 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (Q = It).

hints.prompt: How are current, charge, and time related?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current is charge per unit time, so Q = I·t.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Charge is current per unit time, so Q = I/t.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Charge is time per unit current, so Q = t/I.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Charge equals current plus time.

hints.rationale: Since I = Q/t, the charge is current multiplied by time.

hints.prompt: Which calculation gives the charge for 2.5 A flowing over 0.5 s?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Multiply 2.5 A by 0.5 s.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Divide 2.5 A by 0.5 s.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Divide 0.5 s by 2.5 A.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Add 2.5 and 0.5.

hints.rationale: Q = It, so multiply the current by the time.

hints.prompt: Which common mistake would produce a wrong value for the charge here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dividing the current by the time instead of multiplying them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiplying the current by the time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Using seconds as the unit of time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Using amperes as the unit of current.

hints.rationale: Dividing rather than multiplying is the classic error that yields a wrong distractor.

questions.stem: If the voltage across a fixed resistance is doubled, what happens to the current?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It doubles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It halves.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It stays the same.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The current cannot be determined.

questions.explanation: By Ohm's law, I = V/R. With R fixed, current is directly proportional to voltage, so doubling the voltage doubles the current.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 20 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (Ohm's law, I = V/R).

hints.prompt: According to Ohm's law, how does the current through a fixed resistance depend on the voltage across it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current is directly proportional to voltage (I = V/R).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Current is inversely proportional to voltage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current is independent of voltage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Current is proportional to voltage squared.

hints.rationale: With R fixed, I = V/R makes current proportional to voltage.

hints.prompt: With the resistance held constant, doubling the voltage changes the current by what factor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It multiplies by two.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It multiplies by one-half.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It stays the same.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It multiplies by four.

hints.rationale: Because current is proportional to voltage at fixed R, twice the voltage means twice the current.

hints.prompt: Since R is fixed and stays constant, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'It halves' and 'the current cannot be determined'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'It doubles' and 'it stays the same'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'It stays the same' and 'it cannot be determined'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: A fixed, known resistance means the current is determinable and rises with voltage, ruling out those two options.

questions.stem: Which of the following affects the resistivity of a wire?

choices.label: A
choices.text: length

choices.label: B
choices.text: area of cross section

choices.label: C
choices.text: material

choices.label: D
choices.text: all of the above

questions.explanation: Resistivity is an intrinsic property of the material (and temperature) only. Length and cross-sectional area affect the resistance (R = ρL/A) but not the resistivity ρ itself.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 20 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (resistivity is a material property).

hints.prompt: What kind of property is resistivity — does it depend on an object's size and shape or on the substance itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resistivity is an intrinsic property of the material (and its temperature), not of size or shape.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resistivity depends on the length and cross-sectional area of the object.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resistivity depends only on the object's length.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resistivity depends only on the cross-sectional area.

hints.rationale: Resistivity is a material property, independent of geometry.

hints.prompt: Length and cross-sectional area appear in R = ρL/A. Which quantity do they determine — resistance or resistivity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They affect the resistance R, not the resistivity ρ.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They affect the resistivity ρ, not the resistance R.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They affect both equally.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They affect neither.

hints.rationale: Geometry sets the resistance through R = ρL/A, while ρ stays fixed for the material.

hints.prompt: Since length and area affect resistance but not resistivity, which two answer choices can be ruled out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'length' and 'area of cross section'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'material' and 'all of the above'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'length' and 'material'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'area of cross section' and 'all of the above'

hints.rationale: Length and area are geometric factors, so neither changes the resistivity.

questions.stem: Suppose the resistance of a wire is R. What will be the resistance of another wire of the same material with the same length but double the diameter?

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2R

choices.label: D
choices.text: 4R

questions.explanation: Resistance is R = ρL/A, and cross-sectional area A is proportional to diameter squared. Doubling the diameter quadruples the area, so the resistance drops to one-fourth: R/4.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 20 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (R = ρL/A; A ∝ diameter²).

hints.prompt: How does a wire's resistance depend on its cross-sectional area, and how does area depend on diameter?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Resistance is inversely proportional to area, and area is proportional to diameter squared.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resistance is proportional to area, and area is proportional to diameter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resistance is inversely proportional to the diameter directly.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Resistance does not depend on the diameter.

hints.rationale: R = ρL/A with A ∝ d², so resistance falls as diameter grows.

hints.prompt: Doubling the diameter changes the cross-sectional area by what factor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It multiplies the area by four.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It multiplies the area by two.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It leaves the area unchanged.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It halves the area.

hints.rationale: Area scales with the square of the diameter, so twice the diameter gives four times the area.

hints.prompt: Since a larger diameter increases the area, the resistance must ___, ruling out which options?

choices.label: A
choices.text: decrease, ruling out '2R' and '4R'

choices.label: B
choices.text: increase, ruling out 'R/2' and 'R/4'

choices.label: C
choices.text: stay the same, ruling out all options

choices.label: D
choices.text: increase, ruling out 'R/4' only

hints.rationale: More area means less resistance, so the options larger than R are impossible.

questions.stem: In a circuit, a parallel combination of six 1.6-kΩ resistors is connected in series with a parallel combination of four 2.4-kΩ resistors. If the source voltage is 24 V, what percentage of the total current flows in any one of the 2.4-kΩ resistors?
questions.explanation: The total current must pass through the four-resistor parallel section. Because those four 2.4-kΩ resistors are identical, the current divides equally among them, so each carries one-fourth (25%) of the total current.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (equal split among 4 identical parallel resistors).

hints.prompt: When the entire circuit current passes through a set of identical resistors in parallel, how does the current divide among them?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It splits equally, so each identical resistor carries an equal fraction of the total.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The largest-numbered resistor carries the most current.

choices.label: C
choices.text: All the current goes through just one resistor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The current divides in proportion to each resistance.

hints.rationale: Identical parallel resistors share the current equally.

hints.prompt: The total current flows through the parallel group of four 2.4-kΩ resistors. Since they are identical, each carries what fraction of the total?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One-fourth.

choices.label: B
choices.text: One-sixth.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One-half.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All of it.

hints.rationale: Four identical resistors split the current into four equal parts.

hints.prompt: Which resistor group sets the fraction for one 2.4-kΩ resistor — and what is the trap to avoid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the four resistors in that parallel group, not the six in the other group.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Use the six 1.6-kΩ resistors, since they carry the same current.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Use the total of ten resistors.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Use the two groups combined.

hints.rationale: The 2.4-kΩ resistor's share depends only on how many are in its own parallel group.

questions.stem: A parallel combination of six 1.6-kΩ resistors is in series with a parallel combination of four 2.4-kΩ resistors, with a 24-V source. If the circuit is modified by removing some of the 1.6-kΩ resistors so that the total current becomes 24 mA, how many 1.6-kΩ resistors were removed?
questions.explanation: For a total current of 24 mA at 24 V, the total resistance must be 24 V / 0.024 A = 1000 Ω. The four 2.4-kΩ resistors in parallel give 600 Ω, so the 1.6-kΩ parallel section must be 1000 - 600 = 400 Ω. Since 1600/n = 400 Ω, n = 4 resistors remain, meaning 6 - 4 = 2 were removed.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem made self-contained (restates the setup from the source's previous question). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: For a circuit, how do you find the required total resistance from the source voltage and the total current?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Total resistance = voltage ÷ current (R = V/I).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total resistance = voltage × current.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Total resistance = current ÷ voltage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Total resistance = voltage + current.

hints.rationale: Ohm's law gives the total resistance as voltage divided by current.

hints.prompt: After finding the needed total resistance and subtracting the fixed 600 Ω of the four-2.4-kΩ group, how do you get the number n of 1.6-kΩ resistors that remain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Set 1600 Ω ÷ n equal to the remaining resistance and solve for n.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multiply 1600 Ω by the remaining resistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add 1600 Ω to the remaining resistance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Set 1600 Ω × n equal to the remaining resistance.

hints.rationale: n identical parallel resistors give 1600/n, which must equal the leftover resistance.

hints.prompt: The problem starts with six 1.6-kΩ resistors. Once you solve for the number that remain, what must you do to answer the question?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Subtract the number remaining from six to get the number removed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Report the number remaining directly as the number removed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add the number remaining to six.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Multiply the number remaining by six.

hints.rationale: The question asks how many were removed, which is six minus the number that remain.

questions.stem: Two resistors, with resistances R and 2R, are connected in parallel to a voltage source. If the power dissipated in R is 10 W, what is the power dissipated in 2R?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 1 W

choices.label: C
choices.text: 5 W

questions.explanation: In parallel, both resistors have the same voltage V across them, and P = V²/R. Since the 2R resistor has twice the resistance, it dissipates half the power: 10 W / 2 = 5 W.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem states the parallel configuration explicitly (consistent with the source figure and the only answer choice that is physically consistent). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (parallel: P = V²/R).

hints.prompt: For two resistors in parallel, what quantity is the same across them, and how does dissipated power relate to resistance at fixed voltage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The voltage is the same across both, and P = V²/R, so a larger resistance dissipates less power.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The current is the same through both, and P = I²R, so a larger resistance dissipates less power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The voltage is the same, and P = V²/R, so a larger resistance dissipates more power.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both the voltage and the current are the same across parallel resistors.

hints.rationale: Parallel resistors share voltage, and P = V²/R decreases as resistance increases.

hints.prompt: Since both resistors share the same voltage, how does the power dissipated in the 2R resistor compare with that in R?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is half as much, because P ∝ 1/R at fixed voltage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is twice as much, because it has more resistance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the same, because they are in parallel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is four times as much.

hints.rationale: Doubling the resistance at fixed voltage halves the power V²/R.

hints.prompt: The 2R resistor dissipates less power than R, but not a tiny fraction of it. Which two options can be eliminated as either equal to or far below the expected value?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '10 W' (equal to R) and '1 W' (far too small)

choices.label: C
choices.text: '5 W' and '10 W'

hints.rationale: The 2R power is a modest fraction below 10 W, so 10 W (equal) and 1 W (one-tenth) are both impossible.

questions.stem: Two voltage sources, A and B, have the same emf but different internal resistances—source A's internal resistance is lower than source B's. If they both supply the same current in their circuits, which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: External resistance in source A's circuit is more than in source B's circuit.

choices.label: B
choices.text: External resistance in source A's circuit is less than in source B's circuit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: External resistance in source A's circuit is the same as in source B's circuit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relationship between external resistances in the two circuits can't be determined.

questions.explanation: For each source, emf = I(R_external + r). With equal emf and equal current, the total (R_external + r) must be equal for both. Since source A has the smaller internal resistance r, it must have the larger external resistance to keep the sum the same.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (emf = I(R + r) with equal emf and I).

hints.prompt: For a real voltage source, how does the emf relate to the current, external resistance, and internal resistance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: emf = I(R_external + r), so the sum R_external + r is fixed when emf and current are fixed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: emf = I·R_external only; internal resistance does not matter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: emf = I(R_external − r).

choices.label: D
choices.text: emf = R_external + r, independent of the current.

hints.rationale: The emf drives current through the series sum of external and internal resistance.

hints.prompt: With equal emf and equal current, the sum (R_external + r) is the same for both sources. So the source with the smaller internal resistance must have ___ external resistance.

choices.label: A
choices.text: a larger

choices.label: B
choices.text: a smaller

choices.label: C
choices.text: an equal

choices.label: D
choices.text: a zero

hints.rationale: If the sum is fixed and r is smaller, the external resistance must be larger to compensate.

hints.prompt: Because the sum R_external + r is fixed and the two internal resistances differ, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'External resistances are the same' and 'the relationship cannot be determined'.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A's external resistance is more' and 'A's external resistance is less'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A's is more' and 'they are the same'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: Differing internal resistances force differing external resistances, and the relationship is fully determined.

questions.stem: A battery is connected to a resistor and an initially uncharged capacitor in series. The switch is closed at t = 0. While the capacitor is being charged, which of the following is true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current through and voltage across the resistor increase.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Current through and voltage across the resistor decrease.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current through and voltage across the resistor first increase and then decrease.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Current through and voltage across the resistor first decrease and then increase.

questions.explanation: In an RC charging circuit the current is largest immediately after the switch closes and then decays exponentially toward zero as the capacitor charges. Since the resistor voltage equals IR, it decreases along with the current.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (RC charging current decays exponentially).

hints.prompt: In an RC charging circuit, how does the current behave from the instant the switch closes as the capacitor charges?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The current is largest right after closing and then decays toward zero.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The current starts at zero and rises toward a maximum.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The current stays constant while charging.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The current oscillates up and down.

hints.rationale: RC charging current peaks initially and decays exponentially as the capacitor fills.

hints.prompt: Since the resistor's voltage equals current times resistance (V = IR), how does the resistor voltage change as the current decays?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It decreases along with the current.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It increases as the current decays.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It stays constant while the current decays.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It first rises and then falls.

hints.rationale: With R fixed, the resistor voltage tracks the decaying current downward.

hints.prompt: Because both the resistor current and voltage change the same way during charging, which options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Both increase' and 'first increase then decrease', since the current never rises during charging.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Both decrease' and 'first decrease then increase'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'First increase then decrease' and 'first decrease then increase'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No options can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: The charging current only decays, so any option in which it rises can be ruled out.

questions.stem: A battery, resistor, and initially uncharged capacitor are connected in series and the switch is closed. When the capacitor is fully charged, which of the following is NOT zero?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current in the resistor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Voltage across the resistor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Current in the capacitor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the above (all of those are zero).

questions.explanation: Once the capacitor is fully charged, no current flows, so the current in the resistor and capacitor is zero, and the resistor voltage (IR) is also zero. The only nonzero quantity is the voltage across the capacitor, which is not listed—so none of the listed quantities is nonzero.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 21 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (at full charge, current = 0 ⇒ resistor V = 0; capacitor voltage, not listed, is nonzero).

hints.prompt: When a capacitor in an RC circuit is fully charged, what is the steady-state current in the circuit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero — no current flows once the capacitor is fully charged.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maximum — the current peaks at full charge.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Equal to the battery voltage divided by zero.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constant and nonzero.

hints.rationale: A fully charged capacitor blocks steady current, so the loop current is zero.

hints.prompt: With zero current at full charge, what is the voltage across the resistor (V = IR)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero, because the current is zero.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Equal to the battery voltage.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Equal to the capacitor voltage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nonzero but small.

hints.rationale: No current means no IR drop across the resistor.

hints.prompt: At full charge no current flows anywhere in the series loop. Which two listed quantities are therefore immediately zero?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The current in the resistor and the current in the capacitor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The voltage across the resistor and the current in the capacitor.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The current in the resistor and the voltage across the capacitor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The voltage across the resistor and the voltage across the capacitor.

hints.rationale: Zero current makes both the resistor current and the capacitor current zero.

questions.stem: A bar magnet is oriented so that the north pole of the bar magnet points north. A compass needle is placed to the north of the bar magnet. In which direction does the north pole of the compass needle point?

choices.label: A
choices.text: North

choices.label: B
choices.text: East

choices.label: C
choices.text: South

choices.label: D
choices.text: West

questions.explanation: Just beyond the bar magnet's north pole, the magnet's field points outward (away from that pole), i.e., toward the north. A compass needle aligns its north pole with the local field, so it points north (the needle's north pole is also pushed away from the magnet's like north pole).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (field beyond the N pole points outward; like poles repel).

hints.prompt: Just outside a bar magnet's north pole, which way does the magnetic field point, and how does a compass needle align?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The field points outward, away from the north pole, and a compass needle's north end aligns with the local field.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The field points inward toward the north pole, and the compass aligns against the field.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The field points sideways across the pole.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A compass needle aligns its south end with the field.

hints.rationale: Field lines emerge from a north pole, and a compass north end follows the local field.

hints.prompt: Since the compass sits beyond the magnet's north pole, the magnet's field at the compass points ___.

choices.label: A
choices.text: away from the magnet (outward from the north pole)

choices.label: B
choices.text: back toward the magnet (into the north pole)

choices.label: C
choices.text: perpendicular to the magnet's axis

choices.label: D
choices.text: in a circle around the compass

hints.rationale: Field lines leave a north pole, so at a point beyond it they point outward.

hints.prompt: Because the magnet's field along this line lies on the north-south axis, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'East' and 'West'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'North' and 'South'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'North' and 'East'

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: The field here runs along the north-south line, so the sideways east/west options are impossible.

questions.stem: How will the magnetic domains in an unmagnetized iron rod respond to the presence of a strong external magnetic field?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The domains will split into monopoles.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The domains will tend to align with the external field.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The domains will tend to orient themselves perpendicular to the external field.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The domains will tend to align so as to cancel out the external field.

questions.explanation: In a ferromagnet, an external field causes the magnetic domains (and the dipoles within them) to rotate and grow so that they align with the field, reinforcing it. (Magnetic monopoles do not exist.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (domains align with the applied field).

hints.prompt: How do the magnetic domains in a ferromagnet respond to a strong external magnetic field?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They tend to rotate and grow so as to align with the external field.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They split into magnetic monopoles.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They align so as to cancel the external field.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They orient perpendicular to the external field.

hints.rationale: An applied field makes favorably oriented domains grow and align, magnetizing the material.

hints.prompt: An aligned domain reinforces the applied field, which is why the iron becomes magnetized. This means the domains line up ___ the external field.

choices.label: A
choices.text: parallel to (in the same direction as)

choices.label: B
choices.text: opposite to

choices.label: C
choices.text: perpendicular to

choices.label: D
choices.text: randomly relative to

hints.rationale: Domains align in the same direction as the field, strengthening it.

hints.prompt: Since magnetic monopoles do not exist and alignment (not a perpendicular orientation) is what magnetizes iron, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Split into monopoles' and 'orient perpendicular to the field'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Align with the field' and 'align to cancel the field'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Align with the field' and 'orient perpendicular'

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: Monopoles are impossible and perpendicular alignment would not magnetize the rod, so both are out.

questions.stem: A proton moves in the -x-direction and encounters a uniform magnetic field pointing in the +x-direction. In what direction is the resulting magnetic force on the proton?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The proton experiences no magnetic force.

choices.label: B
choices.text: +x-direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: -y-direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: +y-direction

questions.explanation: The magnetic force is F = qv × B. Here the velocity (-x) and field (+x) are antiparallel, so their cross product is zero. A charge moving parallel or antiparallel to the field feels no magnetic force.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (v parallel/antiparallel to B ⇒ zero force).

hints.prompt: What is the magnetic force on a charge moving parallel or antiparallel to the magnetic field?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Zero, because F = qv×B vanishes when v and B lie along the same line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maximum, because the vectors are aligned.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Directed along the velocity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Directed opposite the field.

hints.rationale: The cross product is zero when velocity and field are collinear, so no force acts.

hints.prompt: Here the velocity is along −x and the field is along +x. How are these two vectors oriented relative to each other?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are antiparallel (pointing in exactly opposite directions along the same axis).

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are perpendicular (at 90°).

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are at 45° to each other.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They point in the same direction (parallel).

hints.rationale: −x and +x lie on the same axis but point oppositely, so they are antiparallel.

hints.prompt: One tempting distractor says the force points in the +x-direction (along the field). Why can that choice be ruled out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A magnetic force is always perpendicular to both the velocity and the field, so it can never point along the +x field direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because the proton is positive, the force must be along +x.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because the field is along +x, the force must also be along +x.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The magnetic force is always parallel to the velocity.

hints.rationale: A magnetic force can never lie along the field or the velocity, so the +x option is a trap.

questions.stem: A straight wire oriented north-south carries current toward the south. The wire is in the Earth's magnetic field, which has a horizontal component pointing north and a vertical component pointing down. What is the direction of the magnetic force felt by the wire?

choices.label: A
choices.text: West

choices.label: B
choices.text: East

choices.label: C
choices.text: Up

choices.label: D
choices.text: North

questions.explanation: The force on a current-carrying wire is F = IL × B. The horizontal (north) field component is antiparallel to the southward current and contributes no force. The downward field component, crossed with the southward current, gives a force toward the east (south × down = east).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified via F = IL × B (only the vertical field component contributes).

hints.prompt: For a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field, how do you find the force, and what happens to a field component parallel or antiparallel to the current?

choices.label: A
choices.text: F = IL×B; a field component along (or opposite to) the current produces no force.

choices.label: B
choices.text: F = IL·B; every field component contributes equally.

choices.label: C
choices.text: F = IL/B; parallel components produce the largest force.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force always points along the current direction.

hints.rationale: Only field components perpendicular to the current contribute to F = IL×B.

hints.prompt: The current points south. Which field component actually produces a force, and which does not?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The downward (vertical) component produces the force; the northward (horizontal) component, being antiparallel to the current, does not.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The northward component produces the force; the downward component does not.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both components produce forces that add along the wire.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither component produces any force.

hints.rationale: The north component is antiparallel to the south current, so only the vertical component matters.

hints.prompt: The magnetic force on the wire is horizontal and perpendicular to the north-south wire. Which two answer choices does that rule out?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'North' (along the wire) and 'Up' (not horizontal)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'East' and 'West'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'East' and 'Up'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'North' and 'West'

hints.rationale: A force perpendicular to the wire and horizontal cannot point along the wire ('North') or vertically ('Up').

questions.stem: A long straight wire along the y-axis carries current in the +y-direction. Taking the xy-plane as the plane of the page (x to the right, y up, z out of the page), in what direction is the magnetic field at a point on the +x-axis near the wire?

choices.label: A
choices.text: away from the wire

choices.label: B
choices.text: vertically upward

choices.label: C
choices.text: into the page

choices.label: D
choices.text: out of the page

questions.explanation: By the right-hand rule for a straight current (grasp the wire with the right thumb along +y), the field circles the wire. On the +x side it points in the -z direction—into the page. (Equivalently, B ∝ Î × r̂ = ŷ × x̂ = -ẑ.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim (coordinate convention from the source preserved). OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by the right-hand rule.

hints.prompt: How do you find the direction of the magnetic field around a long straight current-carrying wire?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the right-hand rule: the field circles the wire, perpendicular to both the current and the radial direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The field points radially away from the wire.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The field points along the current direction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The field points straight toward the wire.

hints.rationale: The field of a straight current forms circles around the wire, perpendicular to current and radius.

hints.prompt: With the current along +y and the point on the +x axis, along which axis does the circulating field point there?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Along the z-axis (perpendicular to the page), from the cross product of the current and radial directions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Along the x-axis (radially).

choices.label: C
choices.text: Along the y-axis (with the current).

choices.label: D
choices.text: At 45° within the xy-plane.

hints.rationale: The field ∝ (current direction)×(radial direction) points along ±z, i.e., into or out of the page.

hints.prompt: The field must be perpendicular to both the current direction and the radial direction. Which two answer choices can be eliminated because they lie along the current or radial directions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'away from the wire' (radial) and 'vertically upward' (along the current)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'into the page' and 'out of the page'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'vertically upward' and 'into the page'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'away from the wire' and 'out of the page'

hints.rationale: The radial ('away') and along-current ('upward') directions cannot be the field, leaving the two out-of-plane options.

questions.stem: Two parallel wires carry equal currents in the same direction and are separated by a small distance. What is the direction of the magnetic force the two wires exert on each other?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No force, since the wires are parallel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No force, since the currents are in the same direction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The force is attractive.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The force is repulsive.

questions.explanation: Parallel wires carrying currents in the same direction attract each other. (Antiparallel currents repel.) Each wire sits in the magnetic field of the other, and applying F = IL × B gives a mutual attractive force.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 22 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (same-direction parallel currents attract).

hints.prompt: What is the magnetic interaction between two parallel wires carrying currents in the same direction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They attract each other.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They repel each other.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They exert no force because they are parallel.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They exert no force because the currents are equal.

hints.rationale: Same-direction parallel currents attract; only antiparallel currents repel.

hints.prompt: Each wire sits in the magnetic field created by the other. Applying F = IL×B to same-direction currents gives a force that pulls them ___.

choices.label: A
choices.text: together

choices.label: B
choices.text: apart

choices.label: C
choices.text: sideways along the wires

choices.label: D
choices.text: not at all

hints.rationale: The mutual F = IL×B forces point inward, drawing the wires together.

hints.prompt: Since two current-carrying wires always exert magnetic forces on each other, which answer choices can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both 'no force' options.

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The force is attractive' and 'the force is repulsive'.

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'No force since parallel' and 'the force is attractive'.

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of the choices can be eliminated.

hints.rationale: Current-carrying wires do exert a mutual force, so both 'no force' choices are out.
